games.overall: Checkers is almost entirely insulated from this finding set. It depends on BoardSmith via `file:../../BoardSmith` and imports only stable, widely-used API surface (engine element/action/flow primitives, GameShell/AutoUI + interaction composables, AI Objective type, testing helpers). None of the dead-code removals (F1-F16) touch a symbol checkers imports — verified against every import in src/ and tests/. All docs-mismatch findings (F17-F30) are documentation-only and require no game code change; checkers already uses the correct runtime APIs (getPlayer/nextAfter/getSnapshot/performAction etc.). Of the pit-of-success findings, the game uses none of the affected surfaces directly: no Deck/Hand (F32), no chooseElements multiSelect (F31 uses single chooseElement), no onSelect (F35), no useDragDrop/triggerChoiceSelect/registerBeforeAutoExecute/actionController.cancel in custom UI (F36-F41 — its board is click-driven), no simulateRandomGames/assertFlowState/createSeededRandom direct calls (F47-F50), no WebSocketMessage/client-SDK usage (F46, F52). The ONE finding that genuinely touches code checkers relies on is F33 (loop maxIterations becoming a loud throw instead of silent completion): checkers sets maxIterations on both its game-loop (500) and move-loop (20). Notably, F42/F43/F44 (snapshot-based restore + pending-action recording) are BENEFICIAL to checkers — it stores critical turn state (continuingPiece, continuingPlayer, hasMovedThisTurn) as non-element Game properties that current action-replay restore drops; toJSON does serialize them, so snapshot-based restore would FIX a latent multi-jump restore bug with no code change required. Net: effectively unaffected, with one small verification item (F33).
games.usesBoardsmithHow: Dependency: package.json declares "boardsmith": "file:../../BoardSmith" (local path link to the engine repo), scripts shell out to `npx boardsmith dev/build/validate`. Imports: src/rules/* import engine primitives from 'boardsmith' (Action, ActionDefinition, Piece, Grid, GridCell, Player, Game, GameOptions, loop, eachPlayer, actionStep, execute, sequence, setVar, FlowDefinition); src/rules/ai.ts imports Objective from 'boardsmith/ai'; src/ui/App.vue imports GameShell + AutoUI from 'boardsmith/ui'; src/ui/components/CheckersBoard.vue imports useBoardInteraction, useFLIP, useFlyingElements, usePlayerStatAnimation, FlyingCardsOverlay, useGameGrid, isMyElement, isOpponentElement, toAlgebraicNotation, GameViewElement, UseActionControllerReturn from 'boardsmith/ui'; tests/game.test.ts imports createTestGame, toDebugString, traceAction, logAvailableActions, diffSnapshots from 'boardsmith/testing'. Custom code: a full custom Vue board (click-to-select piece then click destination; drives the engine via actionController.fill/execute and boardInteraction.selectElement/clear), plus a side-by-side AutoUI panel for comparison, plus custom MCTS AI objectives. Game logic stores multi-jump turn state (continuingPiece/continuingPlayer/hasMovedThisTurn) as plain Game-subclass properties rather than element state.

impacts.requiredChange: After the fix, hitting a loop's maxIterations cap throws/devWarns loudly instead of silently completing. The move-loop (cap 20) is safe under normal play (max ~9 multi-jumps + endTurn). The game-loop (cap 500 rounds) is the real exposure: checkers implements no draw/repetition rule, so a king-shuffle stalemate (notably in AI self-play) could run past 500 rounds and would now throw a runtime error instead of quietly ending. Verify by running long/AI-vs-AI games; if the cap can trip, add a real termination condition (draw-by-repetition / no-progress detection) so the now-loud cap behaves as a true assertion rather than a normal exit, or consciously raise/remove the cap. No API signature change — maxIterations is still accepted.

games.overall: Cribbage is almost entirely insulated from the audit findings. It consumes BoardSmith as a local file:/symlink dependency and imports only the stable public surface: `boardsmith` (Game, Action, flow builders, base elements), `boardsmith/ui` (GameShell, AutoUI, animation composables, action-controller types), `boardsmith/ai` (Objective type), and `boardsmith/testing` (createTestGame + debug helpers). It does NOT import any of the dead-code symbols slated for deletion (no ai-trainer, no designer helpers, no useDragDrop/useBoardInteraction/useHoverHighlight, no GameConnection/GameRunner/GameSession, no replay/scenario/fixtures, no flagged assertions). All docs-mismatch findings (F17-F30) are documentation-only and cannot break game code. All session/runtime hosting findings (F42-F45) and security/utility findings are engine-internal and transparent to the game. The only two findings that touch engine features cribbage distinctively exercises are F31 (chooseElements multiSelect min/max enforcement — cribbage's discard action is the canonical consumer) and F33 (loop maxIterations now failing loud — cribbage sets explicit maxIterations:100 on two loops). Both are low risk: cribbage already submits valid 2-card discards and its loops always terminate via the `while` condition well under the cap, so no code change is expected — but they warrant a quick re-verify. Notably, cribbage stores critical state in plain properties and `this.settings` (runningTotal, currentPlayTurn, dealerPosition, cribbagePhase, _originalHandCardIds, etc.) and its discard is a multiSelect pending action, so it is SENSITIVE to the replay/undo/AI-history semantics in F42/F43/F44 — but those fixes improve correctness transparently and do not force any game-code change.
games.usesBoardsmithHow: Dependency is `"boardsmith": "file:../../BoardSmith"` (package.json), realized as a symlink node_modules/boardsmith -> ../../../BoardSmith. Imports are all from the package's public subpath exports: `boardsmith` (Game, GameOptions, Action, ActionDefinition, loop/actionStep/simultaneousActionStep/execute/sequence/phase flow builders, FlowDefinition/FlowContext, base Card/Hand/Deck/Space/Player), `boardsmith/ui` (GameShell, AutoUI, useFlyingElements, useFLIP, FlyingCardsOverlay, element helpers, UseActionControllerReturn type), `boardsmith/ai` (Objective type for MCTS objectives in ai.ts), and `boardsmith/testing` (createTestGame, toDebugString, traceAction, logAvailableActions, diffSnapshots). Custom UI: src/ui/components/CribbageBoard.vue drives actions via props.actionController.execute('discard'|'playCard'|'sayGo'|'acknowledgeScore', args). Custom AI: rules/ai.ts exports getCribbageObjectives wired through gameDefinition.ai.objectives. Game registers a custom CribbagePlayer class and several element subclasses; heavy use of plain-property + this.settings state for HMR-safety.

impacts.evidence: ~/BoardSmithGames/cribbage/src/rules/actions.ts:1004 (multiSelect: { min: 2, max: 2 }) consumed by src/ui/components/CribbageBoard.vue:520 (actionController.execute('discard', { cards: [...] }))
impacts.requiredChange: No code change expected: the discard UI always submits exactly 2 valid hand-card IDs, so server-side min/max enforcement and the no-silent-drop rule will accept it. Re-verify the discard auto-confirm flow still works after the engine now validates multiSelect length; the redundant cards.length!==2 guard in execute() can stay or be removed.

No code change expected: both loops terminate via their `while` condition (game finished / all cards played) far below 100 iterations, so the new throw-on-cap behavior is never reached in normal play. Confirm no edge case (e.g., a Go-handling stall) can spin the play-loop to the cap, since hitting it will now throw instead of silently completing.

games.overall: Very low impact. This is a thin demo of ActionPanel/AutoUI selection features that consumes the public boardsmith barrel and boardsmith/ui only, with default GameShell slots and no custom AI, no Deck/Hand, no custom UI composables, and no tests. The overwhelming majority of findings are internal dead-code, docs, or library-internal pit-of-success fixes that do not require any change to this game's code. The only finding that can force a change is F33 (loop maxIterations becoming a loud throw), and even that is an edge case for normal play.
games.usesBoardsmithHow: Depends on boardsmith via file:../../BoardSmith (package.json). Imports public engine API from 'boardsmith' (Action, Player, Piece, Space, Game, loop, actionStep, GameOptions, ActionDefinition/ActionContext types) and UI from 'boardsmith/ui' (GameShell, AutoUI). No custom AI. Custom UI is just src/ui/App.vue wrapping GameShell + AutoUI with default-style slots (game-board renders AutoUI; player-stats shows score). Rules in src/rules/: game.ts (TestActionPanelGame extends Game with TestPlayer, builds a Battlefield Space with Unit/Enemy Pieces, registers 8 demo actions, sets a single-loop flow), actions.ts (demo actions for chooseElement, chooseFrom+dependsOn+dynamic multiSelect, optional, disabled, enterNumber, enterText, repeatUntil, playerChoices), elements.ts (Piece/Space/Player subclasses; visibility via showToAll()/contentsVisible()), flow.ts (loop with maxIterations:100 over actionStep). Game type 'test-action-panel', 1-2 players, solo preset.

impacts.requiredChange: After the fix, hitting the maxIterations safety cap throws (or loudly warns) instead of silently completing the loop. This game's whole purpose is letting a user repeatedly click through demo actions (optionalDemo, disabledDemo, enterNumber, etc.) that do NOT defeat enemies, so isFinished() can stay false for well over 100 player-actions. Under the new behavior that exploratory session would now error out at iteration 100. Mitigate by raising maxIterations substantially (or removing the cap / restructuring the while-condition) so the demo's open-ended clicking does not trip the assertion.

games.overall: Lightly affected. This is an animation-showcase sandbox that consumes the engine through stable public APIs (Game/Player/Card/Space/Action/loop/actionStep) and exercises the UI animation composables heavily. None of the dead-code or docs findings touch it. Of the pit-of-success findings, only three matter, and they cluster in the UI layer it leans on: F40 is a concrete break (it registers the same beforeAutoExecute hook twice, relying on the current overwrite semantics), F36 is a real risk because this game IS the canonical custom-UI drag-drop consumer and uses the exact element->element shape ActionPanel auto-wires, and F33 is a latent reliance because the game loop is intentionally infinite and bounded only by maxIterations (silent completion). No engine-state, session, client, AI, or testing findings apply.
games.usesBoardsmithHow: Depends on boardsmith via a local file link: package.json declares "boardsmith": "file:../../BoardSmith" (not vendored/aliased). It imports the public entrypoints only: 'boardsmith' (Game, Player as BasePlayer, GameOptions, Card as BaseCard, Space, Action, ActionDefinition, loop, actionStep, FlowDefinition), 'boardsmith/ui' (GameShell, findElement, useFLIP, useFlyingElements, usePlayerStatAnimation, useActionAnimations, useDragDrop, FlyingCardsOverlay, useAnimationEvents, UseActionControllerReturn type), 'boardsmith/ui/animation/drag-drop.css', and 'boardsmith/testing' (createTestGame in tests). Rules layer (src/rules): DemoGame extends Game with 4 generic Zone (Space subclass) zones holding 16 Card elements; 9 actions each demoing an animation composable, built with Action.create().chooseElement/.chooseFrom/.condition/.execute. Flow is a single-player infinite sandbox: loop({ while: () => !game.isFinished() /* always true */, maxIterations: 10000, do: actionStep(...) }). Custom UI only (no AutoUI, no custom AI): App.vue wraps a custom GameTable.vue in GameShell via the #game-board slot, driving the actionController directly (currentAction/currentPick/validElements/fill/start/cancel/registerBeforeAutoExecute) and using useDragDrop's drag()/drop() helpers plus the flying/FLIP/action-animation/event composables. Run/build/test via npx boardsmith dev|build|validate and vitest.

impacts.evidence: src/ui/components/GameTable.vue:341 and :350 both call props.actionController.registerBeforeAutoExecute(actionAnimations.onBeforeAutoExecute) with the same hook (once in onMounted, once in an immediate watch). Engine confirms overwrite semantics at src/ui/composables/useActionController.ts:741 (beforeAutoExecuteHook.value = hook).
impacts.requiredChange: Today the duplicate registration is harmless because the second call overwrites the first. If the fix renames to setBeforeAutoExecute, both call sites break (method no longer exists). If the fix switches to array-append, the same hook runs twice per action and fires the action animation twice. Either way the game must change: register the hook exactly once (drop the onMounted call or the watch) and/or rename the call.

impacts.evidence: createDragDropAction in src/rules/actions.ts (chooseElement('card') then chooseElement('targetZone')) is an element->element drag-drop, the exact 'Case 3' shape ActionPanel hard-codes (src/ui/components/auto-ui/ActionPanel.vue:879+). GameTable.vue:393 uses useDragDrop and binds drop({name:'zone-a'})/drag({id}) throughout the zone grid, relying on ActionPanel's isDragging watch to derive drop targets and trigger the action.
impacts.requiredChange: The fix moves drop-target derivation out of ActionPanel into the shared board-interaction/controller layer. This game is the primary custom-UI drag-drop consumer and depends on the element->element auto-wiring path being relocated. If the new derivation still exposes drop targets from the targetZone pick's validElements, no code change is needed but the drag-drop demo must be re-verified end-to-end; if semantics shift, the targetZone selection/boardRef wiring may need adjustment.

impacts.evidence: src/rules/flow.ts: loop({ name:'game-loop', while: () => !game.isFinished(), maxIterations: 10000, ... }) combined with src/rules/game.ts isFinished() which always returns false, so the loop is intentionally infinite and bounded only by the maxIterations safety cap.
impacts.requiredChange: F33 changes the maxIterations cap from a silent clean completion into a thrown error. This sandbox relies on the cap as its only terminator, so after 10000 actions the game would throw instead of quietly ending. Reaching that count is improbable in a demo, but the design depends on the silent-cap behavior; the game should give a real termination condition (or raise/relocate the bound) rather than rely on the cap.

games.overall: Very low impact. demo-complex-ui is a pure game-definition + custom-UI consumer of BoardSmith with NO custom AI, NO client/server SDK usage, and NO use of the internal session/runtime/ai-trainer/eslint surfaces that almost every finding touches. The overwhelming majority of findings (F1-F31, F34-F52) are either dead-code removals in modules this game never imports, docs-only fixes, or internal session/runtime/UI-controller behavior changes that leave the game's own code unchanged (e.g. F39 cancel, F42-F45 undo/replay, F43 history recording — all library-internal; the game just consumes actionController.cancel/undo via GameShell slots and is unaffected). Only two findings plausibly require attention, both trivial: F32 (default visibility flip makes the game's explicit hand.contentsVisibleToOwner() redundant and silently hides the deck by default) and F33 (loop maxIterations cap going from silent-complete to throw, against the game's explicit maxIterations:1000 safety cap). Neither breaks normal play; both warrant a quick confirm.
games.usesBoardsmithHow: Depends on BoardSmith via a local file link: package.json declares "boardsmith": "file:../../BoardSmith" and node_modules/boardsmith is a symlink to ../../../BoardSmith. Consumes three public entrypoints by bare specifier: (1) "boardsmith" for engine rules — Game, Player, Action, Card/Hand/Deck/Space base elements, loop/eachPlayer/actionStep flow builders, GameOptions/ActionDefinition/FlowDefinition types (src/rules/*.ts); (2) "boardsmith/ui" for GameShell, AutoUI, UseActionControllerReturn, and view helpers findPlayerHand/findElement/getElementCount/findAllHands/useFlyingElements/FlyingCardsOverlay (src/ui/App.vue, src/ui/components/GameTable.vue); (3) "boardsmith/testing" for createTestGame (tests/game.test.ts). The custom UI (GameTable.vue) drives interaction through actionController only — currentAction/currentPick/currentArgs/validElements/getChoices/fill/start/cancel — the documented "pit of success" controller path; it does NOT use useDragDrop, useBoardInteraction, useHoverHighlight, or triggerChoiceSelect. No custom AI, no client SDK, no server/worker, no ai-trainer usage. Game logic: 5 simultaneously-available actions (collect/discard/trade/gift/score) using chooseElement (single) and chooseFrom; a loop({maxIterations:1000})>eachPlayer>actionStep flow; player.score as a plain (non-element) property; hands made owner-visible explicitly via player.hand.contentsVisibleToOwner().

impacts.evidence: ~/BoardSmithGames/demo-complex-ui/src/rules/game.ts:537 calls player.hand.contentsVisibleToOwner(); deck created at game.ts:541 with NO visibility set (relies on current fully-visible default)
impacts.requiredChange: After Hand defaults to owner-only, the explicit hand.contentsVisibleToOwner() call becomes redundant and can be removed (no break). After Deck defaults to hidden, the deck's contents become hidden in gameView where they are currently fully visible; the custom UI is fine (GameTable shows only deckCount via getElementCount, and hidden zones still emit placeholder children + childCount, verified in engine/element/game.ts:2236-2264), but the side-by-side AutoUI panel will now render the deck face-down. Confirm that visual change is acceptable for the demo; no functional code change is forced.

impacts.evidence: ~/BoardSmithGames/demo-complex-ui/src/rules/flow.ts:191-194 — loop({ name:'game-loop', while:()=>!game.isFinished(), maxIterations:1000, ... }); engine currently silently completes at the cap (flow/engine.ts:1034-1037)
impacts.requiredChange: The game relies on isFinished() as the real terminator and treats maxIterations:1000 as a silent safety cap. After the fix the cap throws instead of completing cleanly. Normal 2-player play ends well under the cap, but pathological loops (e.g. players repeatedly trading without depleting the deck) that currently end silently would now raise. Confirm the demo always terminates via isFinished() before 1000 iterations, or raise/justify the cap.

games.overall: Go-fish is almost entirely insulated from this audit batch. It depends on boardsmith via a local file: link and consumes the engine, ui, ai, and testing surfaces, but it stays on the well-trodden paths: choice-based chooseFrom actions (not chooseElements multiSelect), click selection via actionController.fill (not useDragDrop/triggerChoiceSelect), explicitly-set zone visibility, and the objectives AI hook. Every dead-code finding (F1-F16) targets modules/exports it never imports; every docs finding (F17-F30) only fixes documentation to match the real API the game already uses correctly; and the pit-of-success findings overwhelmingly target APIs it doesn't use (chooseElements bounds F31, onSelect F35, useDragDrop F36/F37/F41, triggerChoiceSelect F38, registerBeforeAutoExecute F40, client SDK F46, random-simulation F49/F50, assertFlowState F47, eslint rules F51, WebSocketMessage F52). F48 (seed required) is a no-op for go-fish because the engine's Game constructor has its own seed-fallback at game.ts:402 and its own createSeededRandom — the utils/random.ts function being changed is only used by ai-trainer. F32 (secure-by-default Deck/Hand visibility) does not force a change because go-fish already explicitly calls contentsHidden()/contentsVisibleToOwner()/contentsVisible() on every zone; the fix merely makes those calls redundant. The ONE finding that touches a surface go-fish actively configures is F33 (loop maxIterations now throws instead of silently completing) — and even there, go-fish's loops terminate via their while-condition long before the safety cap, so no real-world break is expected. Net: zero forced code changes; one finding worth a confirmation pass. Worth noting (not a forced change): F43's pending-action recording gap is exactly the action shape go-fish uses (its two-step "ask"), so the fix improves go-fish's undo/replay/AI-history correctness transparently without requiring game edits.
games.usesBoardsmithHow: Local source dependency: package.json declares "boardsmith": "file:../../BoardSmith". Scripts shell out to npx boardsmith (dev/build/validate). Imports: src/rules/* import from the root 'boardsmith' barrel (Game, Player, GameOptions, BaseCard/BaseHand/Deck/Space, Action/ActionDefinition, flow builders loop/eachPlayer/actionStep/execute/ifThen/sequence/setVar, FlowDefinition, FlowState) and 'boardsmith/ai' (Objective, BotMove, ThreatResponse). src/ui/* import from 'boardsmith/ui' (GameShell, AutoUI, injectBoardInteraction, findElement/findElements/findPlayerHand/findAllHands/getElementCount, useFlyingElements, FlyingCardsOverlay, prefersReducedMotion, flyToPlayerStat, getSuitSymbol/getSuitColor/getRankName, GameElement, UseActionControllerReturn). tests import from 'boardsmith/testing' (createTestGame, simulateAction, assertActionSucceeds/Fails, toDebugString, traceAction, diffSnapshots, logAvailableActions, TestGame). Custom UI: GameTable.vue drives a multi-step chooseFrom action through actionController.fill('target'/'rank', ...), getChoices, currentAction/currentPick/currentArgs, and reads boardInteraction via injectBoardInteraction. Custom AI: src/rules/ai.ts exports getGoFishObjectives (wired into gameDefinition.ai.objectives) plus extra MCTS hooks (threat-response, playout policy, move ordering, UCT). Game registers a single 'ask' action built with Action.create().chooseFrom(...).chooseFrom(...).condition(...).execute(...), and explicitly sets zone visibility (pond.contentsHidden, hand.contentsVisibleToOwner, books.contentsVisible).

impacts.requiredChange: Behavior of loop()'s maxIterations changes from silent completion to throwing/loud-warning when the cap is hit (engine/flow/engine.ts:1035). Go-fish must confirm both loops always exit via their while-condition (turn-loop: turnEnded/isFinished/empty-hand; game-loop: !isFinished) before the cap — which they do in correct play, so most likely no code change is needed, but the safety caps now behave like assertions rather than graceful exits and should be treated as unreachable. No edit required unless a degenerate game (e.g. pond+hands exhausted without 13 books) could legitimately run a loop to its cap, in which case the thrown error would need handling.

games.overall: Hex is almost entirely unaffected. It is the simplest reference game and consumes only the stable core of BoardSmith: Game/Player/Piece/HexGrid/HexCell, the Action builder (single chooseElement), the loop/eachPlayer/actionStep flow builders, AI hook TYPES from boardsmith/ai (Objective/BotMove/ThreatResponse), UI shell + composables (GameShell, AutoUI, useBoardInteraction, useHexGrid, useFLIP, useElementChangeTracker, isMyElement/isOpponentElement), and a few testing helpers (createTestGame, toDebugString, traceAction, logAvailableActions, diffSnapshots). All 35 docs-mismatch/dead-code findings (F1-F30) target internal modules, unused exports, or documentation hex never imports — zero code impact. The pit-of-success findings (F31-F52) also miss hex: it uses click-based interaction (no useDragDrop, so F36/F37/F41 N/A), no Hand/Deck (F32), no onSelect/multiSelect/chooseElements (F31/F35), no 'unordered' visibility (F34), no client SDK or WebSocketMessage (F46/F52), no random-simulation/assertFlowState/createSeededRandom in its tests (F47/F48/F49/F50), no eslint config and no node:net imports (F51), and the GameSession/runtime restore/undo/errorCode internals (F42-F45) are library-internal and never called directly. The only finding touching a surface hex configures is F33 (loop maxIterations becoming a hard throw), and even there hex's loop exits via while=false long before the cap. Net: no required code changes; F33 warrants a 30-second confirmation only.
games.usesBoardsmithHow: Depends on BoardSmith via local path: package.json declares "boardsmith": "file:../../BoardSmith" (not vendored, not aliased). Scripts shell out to the CLI (npx boardsmith dev/build/validate) with no flags. Imports use four entry points: 'boardsmith' (Game, GameOptions, Player, Piece, HexGrid, HexCell, Action, ActionDefinition, loop, eachPlayer, actionStep, FlowDefinition), 'boardsmith/ai' (type-only: Objective, BotMove, ThreatResponse), 'boardsmith/ui' (GameShell, AutoUI, findElement, getElementOwner, useBoardInteraction, prefersReducedMotion, useFLIP, useElementChangeTracker, useHexGrid, isMyElement, isOpponentElement, GameViewElement, UseActionControllerReturn), and 'boardsmith/testing' (createTestGame, toDebugString, traceAction, logAvailableActions, diffSnapshots). Custom game: HexGame extends Game with custom HexPlayer (static PlayerClass), Board extends HexGrid, Cell extends HexCell, Stone extends Piece. Single action placeStone built with Action.create().prompt().chooseElement({elementClass, filter, display, boardRef}).execute(). Flow is loop(while)->eachPlayer->actionStep. Custom UI (HexBoard.vue) is click-based via actionController.execute('placeStone',{cell:id}); it uses useBoardInteraction for highlight parity and useHexGrid for geometry, NOT useDragDrop. Also renders AutoUI side-by-side. Custom AI (ai.ts) exports objectives/threatResponseMoves/playoutPolicy/moveOrdering/uctConstant consumed via gameDefinition.ai. Game options/colorPalette declared in boardsmith.json.

impacts.requiredChange: After the fix makes loop() throw (instead of silently completing) when maxIterations is hit, confirm hex's game-loop still exits cleanly via while=false. It does in normal play: a size-N board fills at N*N cells (49 for default 7x7) -> ~25 loop iterations, far under the 100 cap, so isFinished() returns true and while returns false before the cap. No code change expected; this is a verify-only item. Optionally the now-redundant maxIterations:100 could stay as a harmless safety net.

games.overall: Almost entirely unaffected by the catalog. The game consumes BoardSmith through stable, widely-used surfaces (Game/Player/Die/DicePool/Space, Action builder with chooseFrom/chooseElement, flow loop/eachPlayer/actionStep, Track/AbilityManager, and UI GameShell/AutoUI/Die3D/useFLIP/injectBoardInteraction). It uses none of the dead-code targets (no Deck/Hand, no useDragDrop, no useHoverHighlight, no chooseElements multiSelect, no onSelect, no triggerChoiceSelect, no testing fixtures/assertFlowState, no parallel-trainer). All docs-mismatch findings are documentation-only and force no code change. The single real exposure is F42: the game is architected around replay-based restore (Track/AbilityManager state rebuilt via TRACK_ADD commands; plain score-sheet fields rebuilt by re-running action.execute). Switching GameSession.restore to snapshot-authoritative fromSnapshot both (a) crashes immediately because toJSON serializes getter-only derived fields that fromJSON blindly re-assigns, and (b) would silently lose all Track/AbilityManager state since those private (_-prefixed) objects aren't restored without replay. This is a genuine, verified break.
games.usesBoardsmithHow: Dependency is a local file: link — package.json: "boardsmith": "file:../../BoardSmith". Imports use three entry points: 'boardsmith' (engine: Game, Player, Die, DicePool, Space, AbilityManager, MonotonicTrack, CounterTrack, Track, Action, loop/eachPlayer/actionStep/execute/sequence, plus types DieSides/TrackEntry/TrackCommandEmitter/Ability), 'boardsmith/ui' (GameShell, AutoUI, Die3D, useFLIP, injectBoardInteraction, DIE_ANIMATION_CONTEXT_KEY, createDieAnimationContext, UseActionControllerReturn), and 'boardsmith/testing' (createTestGame, toDebugString, traceAction, logAvailableActions, diffSnapshots). Structure: src/rules (game.ts, elements.ts, actions.ts, flow.ts, index.ts) defines a custom PolyPotionsGame + custom PolyPotionsPlayer + custom elements; src/ui has a custom Vue UI (App.vue shows a custom GameTable/DiceShelf/ScoreSheet side-by-side with AutoUI, wiring through actionController and injectBoardInteraction for shared-state parity). No custom AI module — it only declares gameDefinition.ai = { objectives: () => ({}) } so the built-in MCTS bot can run. Notably the game routes all Track mutations through game.execute({type:'TRACK_ADD'}) commands and rebuilds plain score-sheet state inside action.execute callbacks, i.e. it depends on command/action replay for restore. Tests in tests/game.test.ts use createTestGame.

impacts.evidence: src/rules/elements.ts:1045-1063 PolyPotionsPlayer.toJSON() writes getter-only derived props (json.attributes.score/abilities/distillations/fulminates/poisonSkulls); private Track/AbilityManager state is _-prefixed (_poisonTrack/_distillationTracks/_fulminateTrack/_abilityManager) and only rebuilt via TRACK_ADD commands in src/rules/elements.ts:1019-1036; plain game state (round/craftedValue/craftedPoison/gameEndTriggered in src/rules/game.ts:66,88-97) is rebuilt by re-running action.execute. Verified: GameRunner.fromSnapshot of a fresh game throws 'Cannot set property score of [object Object] which has only a getter' at game-element.ts:934.
impacts.requiredChange: Make the game survive a toJSON/fromJSON snapshot round-trip without action/command replay. Stop serializing getter-only derived values (score/abilities/distillations/fulminates/poisonSkulls) into attributes, and add real (de)serialization for the Track and AbilityManager objects so their entries are restored from the snapshot instead of from replayed TRACK_ADD commands. Today none of that survives fromSnapshot, so the game must change before F42's snapshot-authoritative GameSession.restore can host it.

games.overall: MERC is the most complex consumer: ~55 import sites across boardsmith, boardsmith/ui, boardsmith/testing, boardsmith/runtime, boardsmith/utils, boardsmith/session, boardsmith/ai. Almost all dead-code findings (F1-F16) and all docs findings (F17-F30) are unaffected — MERC imports none of the dead exports (no allOf/anyOf/namedSequence, no useHoverHighlight, no fixtures/ScenarioBuilder, no ai-trainer, no client SDK) and docs-only fixes never force code changes. The risk is concentrated in the pit-of-success behavior changes that touch surfaces MERC heavily uses: deck/hand visibility (F32), flow loop semantics (F33), chooseElements multiSelect (F31), and action-history/replay used by its MCTS clone test (F43). MERC never sets element visibility explicitly, relies on capped flow loops throughout flow.ts, and uses multiSelect min/max in combat. Every affected fix additionally requires re-packing the vendored tarball (vendor/boardsmith-0.0.1-audit-F23_F28.tgz) and re-installing — which raises effort one notch versus a live file: dependency. Net: a handful of likely/affected behavior findings, no dead-code or docs breakage.
games.usesBoardsmithHow: MERC depends on BoardSmith as a VENDORED, PRE-PACKED tarball, not a live file: link to the source tree. package.json pins "boardsmith": "file:./vendor/boardsmith-0.0.1-audit-F23_F28.tgz" in both dependencies and an overrides block; package-lock.json resolves it to file:vendor/boardsmith-0.0.1-audit-F23_F28.tgz and it is installed into node_modules/boardsmith. The tarball name encodes that prior audit fixes (F23/F28) were already baked in by re-packing. Consumption surface: src/rules/* (game.ts MERCGame extends Game, elements.ts extends Card/Piece/Space/Deck/Hand/Grid/GridCell, actions/* using Action/ActionContext/ActionDefinition/dependentFilter, flow.ts using loop/sequence/actionStep with many maxIterations caps); src/ui/* Vue components using GameShell, useDragDrop, useBoardInteraction, useActionController (registerBeforeAutoExecute), GameOverlay, Die3D, animation composables; tests/* using boardsmith/testing (createTestGame, simulateAction, traceAction), boardsmith/runtime (GameRunner + getSnapshot/actionHistory), and boardsmith/ai (createBot in mcts-clone.test only); boardsmith/session createColorOption; boardsmith/utils easing fns. MERC has its own custom AI (src/rules/ai-executor.ts), not the engine MCTS bot. Crucially, MERC NEVER calls setZoneVisibility/contentsVisible on any Deck/Hand — it relies entirely on BoardSmith's current permissive default. Any BoardSmith fix that lands must be re-packed into a new tarball and re-installed (npm install of the file: tgz) before MERC can pick it up.

impacts.evidence: src/rules/elements.ts:1449-1461 declares MercDeck/EquipmentDeck/TacticsDeck extends BaseDeck and TacticsHand extends BaseHand; src/rules/game.ts:894-904 creates merc/weapons/armor/accessories decks; grep shows ZERO setZoneVisibility/contentsVisible calls anywhere in src/rules. UI reads contents from per-player state: src/ui/components/GameTable.vue:466-478 (findByClassName('TacticsHand')), src/ui/components/DictatorPanel.vue:472/563-565 (renders tacticsHand cards), src/ui/composables/useVictoryCalculations.ts:140-145 (counts TacticsDeck/TacticsHand cards).
impacts.requiredChange: MERC relies on the permissive default (decks/hands fully visible). After the fix flips Deck->hidden and Hand->owner-only, MERC must explicitly opt the zones it displays back into the needed visibility (e.g. make TacticsDeck count visible for useVictoryCalculations, ensure TacticsHand visibility matches intended viewers) and re-verify every UI panel that reads deck/hand contents from per-player snapshots. Then re-pack the vendored tarball and re-install.

impacts.evidence: src/rules/flow.ts has 20+ loops with safety caps (maxIterations: 50/20/10/5/1000/200), and at least one where the cap doubles as control flow: flow.ts:744-747 coordinated-attack-response loop has while: () => game.coordinatedAttack !== null with maxIterations: 1; flow.ts:749-751 epinephrine loop maxIterations: 10.
impacts.requiredChange: F33 makes a hit maxIterations cap THROW instead of silently completing. MERC must audit every capped loop in flow.ts to confirm none reach the cap as a normal exit (especially the maxIterations:1 coordinated-attack loop), and raise/restructure any that do. Re-pack and re-install the vendored tarball after the engine change.

impacts.evidence: src/rules/actions/rebel-combat.ts:213,526,744,1436,1750 and src/rules/actions/rebel-economy.ts:111 use multiSelect returning { min: 1, max: N } (e.g. rebel-combat.ts:213-218 target selection min:1/max:pending.maxTargets); execute() at rebel-combat.ts:223+ consumes args.targets as raw string IDs.
impacts.requiredChange: F31 starts enforcing multiSelect min/max server-side and rejecting unresolved element IDs (previously silently dropped). MERC's custom AI (src/rules/ai-executor.ts) and auto-resolve test helpers must always submit selections that satisfy min/max and contain only currently-valid IDs, or actions that previously passed leniently will now be rejected. Audit arg generation, then re-pack/re-install.

impacts.evidence: tests/mcts-clone.test.ts:3 imports createBot from boardsmith/ai and at :304-311 builds the bot from runner.actionHistory; the test header at :116-121 explicitly documents the recording gap ('actions ... recorded in NEITHER commandHistory NOR actionHistory') and :539-546 expects replay/flow divergence. MERC has many pending/multi-step combat actions whose completions are the ones F43 would start recording.
impacts.requiredChange: F43 routes pending/repeating-selection action completions into runner.actionHistory, changing both its length and replay/clone behavior. MERC's MCTS clone diagnostic test encodes the current (gappy) semantics as expected, so its assumptions and assertions will need updating; verify the custom AI/state-persistence paths still restore correctly. Re-pack and re-install.

If the fix renames registerBeforeAutoExecute -> setBeforeAutoExecute (the suggested cleanest option), MERC must rename its single call site in App.vue. If instead it becomes array-based, MERC is unaffected. Trivial code edit but still requires re-packing/re-installing the vendored tarball.

impacts.evidence: src/rules/actions/dictator-actions.ts:2533 onSelect: (value) => { selectedItemIndex = parseInt(value.split(':')[0], 10); }; src/rules/actions/rebel-equipment.ts:89 tracks onSelectUnitId via onSelect.
impacts.requiredChange: F35 makes a throwing onSelect abort the action (fire onCancel, return failure) instead of swallowing and committing. MERC must ensure its onSelect callbacks are exception-safe (the parseInt/split path is fine for well-formed values, but any malformed input that throws would now cancel the action). Verify each onSelect, then re-pack/re-install.

impacts.evidence: src/ui/components/AssignToSquadPanel.vue:3,41 uses useDragDrop() (drag, drop, isOver) for custom drag-and-drop; src/ui/components/GameTable.vue:52 uses useBoardInteraction().
impacts.requiredChange: F36 moves drop-target derivation into the shared controller/board-interaction layer that useDragDrop consumes. MERC's custom drag panels do not use AutoUI's ActionPanel (the hard-coded path), but they do consume useDragDrop/useBoardInteraction directly, so MERC must re-verify that AssignToSquadPanel drag/drop still highlights and accepts the correct targets under the refactored derivation. Re-pack/re-install and exercise the drag UI.
